coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted one-pot synthesis of carbon dots from biomass: Fe(III) ion sensing and in-situ methylene blue reduction catalytic activity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Inc.
coredata.dc:creator: George, Hema Sindhuja
Selvaraj, Hosimin
Ilangovan, Andivelu
Almutairi, Bader O.
Sheit, Mohamed Kasim
Sathishkumar, Kuppusamy
coredata.dc:description: In this study, zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized from affordable organic bio-precursors derived from chayote seeds using a one-pot microwave-assisted carbonization process. These biomass-derived carbon dots display excellent fluorescence properties, with an average size of approximately 3.3 nm and a fluorescence quantum yield about 9.56 %. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at different excitation peaks, with the highest intensity emission peak observed at 387 nm with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. These carbon dots effectively detect Fe3+ ions, with a detection limit of 4.5 µmol. Carbon dots show strong catalytic reduction activity against a reduction of Methylene Blue dye, achieving an 81 % reduction in a short period. These biomass-derived carbon dots presents sustainable and promising option for applications in metal sensing and catalytic degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Biomass
Precursor
Metal sensing
Catalytic reduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the microwave-assisted synthesis of CCDs.

a) UV–Visible spectrum of CCDs. Inside picture - brown and blue colour carbon dots in the presence of normal and UV light (365 nm). b) Fluorescence spectrum of CCDs with various excitation wavelengths (270–400 nm). c) Fluorescence emission wavelength shifts towards the red region d) FTIR spectra of CCDs.
a) HRTEM image of the CCDs b) Average size of the CCDs particles.
a) Fluorescence spectra for various metal ions with CCDs. b) F/F0 and metal ion concertation relationship.
a) Fluorescence spectra for various Fe3+ ion concentrations with CCDs. b) linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ ion concentrations.
Methylene blue absorbance spectra with NaBH4 b) Methylene blue absorbance spectra with NaBH4 and CCDs.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted one-pot synthesis of carbon dots from biomass: Fe(III) ion sensing and in-situ methylene blue reduction catalytic activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized from affordable organic bio-precursors derived from chayote seeds using a one-pot microwave-assisted carbonization process. These biomass-derived carbon dots display excellent fluorescence properties, with an average size of approximately 3.3 nm and a fluorescence quantum yield about 9.56 %. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at different excitation peaks, with the highest intensity emission peak observed at 387 nm with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. These carbon dots effectively detect Fe3+ ions, with a detection limit of 4.5 µmol. Carbon dots show strong catalytic reduction activity against a reduction of Methylene Blue dye, achieving an 81 % reduction in a short period. These biomass-derived carbon dots presents sustainable and promising option for applications in metal sensing and catalytic degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots derived from natural precursors are the recent incomers in the research field of nanomaterials. Carbon dots were initially synthesized by Xu et. al in 2004 with a quasi-spherical shape of size less than 10 nm [24]. Carbon dots possess some unique features like photostability, biocompatibility, reproducibility and solubility which made them utilized in various fields like bioimaging, sensing, agriculture, supercapacitors, photocatalysis, medicine etc [13,18,21,3]., CDs were prepared from chemical substances such as ethylene glycol, citric acid, polyethylene imine and other organic solvents giving toxic products which led to big environmental issues in turn, researchers have concentrated on avoiding toxic chemical usage for the synthesis of carbon dots. Alternatively, researchers use naturally occurring organic resources such as flowers [23], seeds [15], plants [16], leaves [20] etc which contain abundant carbon content through alkaloids, flavonoids and heterocyclic compounds, chemical-based precursors [10,12]. The carbon dots synthesis process is mainly classified into two ways, top-down and bottom-up approach. Generally, a bottom-up approach is used in the hydrothermal and microwave processes to synthesise carbon dots [14]. The microwave method is one of the simple methods of producing eco-friendly and nontoxic carbon dots. The microwave method gathers significant attention in the scientific community because of its low reaction times, environment-friendly, uniform heating energy efficiency etc [17]. Natural organic macromolecules undergo the carbonization process in the presence of microwaves in either acidic or alkaline conditions. Fluorescent carbon dots were synthesised by a microwave-assisted method using papaya seeds as an organic precursor [8].

Carbon dots have gained significant attention in heavy metal sensing applications due to their excellent fluorescence properties. Metal ions such as Hg2+, Al³ ⁺, Fe³ ⁺, Pb²⁺, and Au⁺ can be detected using carbon dots in an aqueous medium using the fluorescence probe method [11]. Carbon dots are more sensitive materials that specifically select only one type of metal ion during the detection process. Fe³ ⁺ ions play an important role in biological activities such as oxygen transport, electron transport, immune response, and enzyme function[6]. The concentration level of Fe³ ⁺ ions is crucial in biological activities; any increase or decrease can affect the entire biological system. Fe³ ⁺ ions are present in the liver, spleen, and bone marrow cells in the human body. Changes in the concentration level of Fe³ ⁺ ions can cause various health problems, including cancer and anaemia. Monitoring and detecting Fe³ ⁺ ion concentration is therefore very important. Various metal sensors and instruments are available to detect metal ion concentration, but the carbon dots fluorescence probe method is a simple and low-cost technique. Most biomass-derived carbon dots are used for metal ion detection sensing applications [4]. Similarlly organic dyes are widely used in various industries such as textiles, food, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics [2]. These industries release a significant amount of unused organic dyes as effluent, which creates environmental and human health problems [19]. Remediation processes are needed to treat or prevent these pollutants from entering the effluent. Many techniques are available to treat and recover useful products from the effluent [2,5,7,9,19]. Recently, nanocatalysts have emerged as one of the best materials for treating methylene blue dye through simple chemical reactions [1]. Carbon dots are also used as catalysts to treat various dyes, such as carmine dye, Rhodamine B dye, methylene blue, and methyl orange. Carbon dots are small in size with a large surface area and high surface activity. Biomass-derived doped and undoped carbon dots can be used to treat organic dyes [22].

In this current work, we have described the synthesis of carbon dots focusing on the biowaste organic precursor i.e. Chayote seeds (Chow chow seeds) which is normally considered as a bio waste while cooking. These seeds are renewable, inexpensive and green resources which produce bright blue fluorescence carbon dots and meet the urgent need for large-scale synthesis of CDs. Generally, the carbon dots require strong acids or surface modification agents to enhance the photoluminescent nature and water solubility whereas the microwave synthesis of carbon dots from Chayote seeds does not require strong acids and surface modification agents. The synthesized water-soluble carbon dots were investigated for the sensing of Fe3+ metal ions and catalytic activity against organic dye.

Chayote seeds were collected from the local market at Trichy, Tamil Nadu, India. The Chayote seeds were dried in sunlight to remove the moisture and grained into a fine powder which was stored in an air tight sterile container. Ortho Phosphoric acid, Sodium hydroxide and Quinine sulfate were purchased from Merck. Ethanol and metal salts from SRL. Double distilled water has been used for the entire research work. All chemicals were used as such without any purification.

The synthesis procedure was followed by a previous research article [8]. 1 g of Chayote seed powder was mixed with about 10 ml of 40 % orthophosphoric acid, stirred well and kept in a domestic microwave oven (LG, Model: MS2043DB) for about 90 s for the carbonization process. The reaction mixture turned dark brown colour due to carbonization and 30 ml of ethanol was added with constant stirring for 10 min. The carbonization product was filtered through the filter paper and then neutralised with sodium hydroxide solution drop by drop. The solution was again filtered using a 0.2 µm syringe filter and the supernatant liquid was collected and named Chayote carbon dots (CCDs) which were used for further characterization techniques. The schematic representation of the CCDs synthesis procedure is depicted in Fig. 1.

Determination of the functional groups in the CCDs were determined by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer JASCO FT/IR-6600 with a wavelength range from 4000 to 400 cm −1 with a scan rate of 64. UV–Visible spectra (UV-1800 SHIMADZU) were recorded and determine the absorbance wavelength of the CCDs. A pinch amount of CCDs was taken in 5 ml quartz cuvette with 3 ml of distilled water (solvent) and shaken well. The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a JASCO FP-8300 spectrometer with a slit range of 5 nm and wavelength range from 290–600 nm. Particle morphology and size were measured using HRTEM (JEM-2100 Plus). The average size particles were measured using the ImageJ software tool and a photographic image was taken by Moto e40 mobile phone.

Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was calculated using the following equation.
(1) Qs = Qref (IS /IRef) (ARef /AS) (ηS/ηRef)
Where Qs and Qref are the quantum yields of synthesised carbon dots and standard (0.1 M H2SO4 as a solvent, Qs =0.546 at 360 nm)), Is and IRef are the integral areas of the fluorescence spectrum of the sample and standard, and ARef and AS is the absorption intensity area of sample and standard where η refers the solvent refractive index.

10 mmol concentration of the freshly prepared metal ions solutions such as Cr3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Mg2+,Pb2+, Fe3+ and Zn2+. 0.5 ml of CCDs and 2 ml metal ion solution were mixed in 5 ml glass vial and kept for 5 mins. The mixture solution was kept under UV light @ 365 nm and also recorded the fluorescence spectra for all metal ion solutions. The quenching behaviour of the CCDs was also examined using various concentrations of the Ferric ion solutions such as 0–50 µmol. The static and dynamic quenching relationship was expressed using the Stern –Volmer Equation.
(2) F0/F = 1+ Ksv[Q]
Where F and F0 are fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of metal ion solutions. Ksv- Stern Volmer quenching constant, Q is metal ion concentration. Newly synthesised CCDs sensing limit of detection (LOD) estimated using the below formula
(3)
LOD =
3 σ
s
Where s is the slope of the calibration plot, σ is the standard deviation of the signals.

The catalytic reduction property of CCDs was examined using UV–visible spectroscopy. Two types of experiments were performed in Methylene blue (MB) reduction process. One is 2.5 ml methylene blue(3 g/L) + 0.5 ml of (0.01 M) sodium borohydride (NaBH4) taken in the 5 ml Quartz cuvette and recorded in situ UV-Spectra for every 2 mins interval. The second one is 2.5 ml methylene blue(3 g/L) + 0.5 ml of (0.01 M) sodium borohydride (NaBH4) with 500 µl CCDs taken in the 5 ml Quartz cuvette and recorded in situ UV-Spectra for every 2 mins interval. In both experiments, freshly prepared NaBH4 was used. The experiment was repeated in triplicate.
Catalytic reduction efficiency is calculated using the following equation.

Catalytic reduction efficiency (%) =
C
i
−
C
f
C
i
X 100.
Where Ci is the initial and Cf final concentration(absorbance) of Methylene blue.

CCDs were synthesised by an ecofriendly and rapid microwave-assisted process. Seed powder was carbonaty microwave and converted into valuable carbon dots which was confirmed by the appearance of dark-brown colour. The water-soluble CCDs were collected and stored at below 10ᵒC. Synthesised carbon dots were characterized by following techniques. Fig. 2(a) shows the UV–Visible spectrum of synthesised CCDs. Here, one major peak was obtained at 270 nm which corresponds to the CC bond with π-π * transition. CCDs show a bright blue colour under 365 nm UV light and a brown colour under normal light which is shown in the inside picture of Fig. 2(a). Based on this absorbance spectra were taken from fluorescence spectra for CCDs. Fig. 2(b) shows the fluorescence spectra of CCDs with various excitation wavelengths from 270 - 400 nm. Each excitation wavelength gave respective emission peaks, while increasing the excitation wavelength the emission intensity peaks also increased and at 320 nm excitation wavelength showed maximum emission intensity peaks at 387 nm after that the intensity of the peak gradually decreased for more than 320 nm excitation wavelength. 320 nm was the exact excitation wavelength of the synthesised CCDs. The emission wavelength gradually increased from (387- 462 nm) which denoted that the fluorescence spectra moved towards the red region which is called Redshift shown in Fig. 2(c). FT-IR spectrum of CCDs shows broad absorption peaks at 3436 cm−1 which can be attributed to the O–H vibrations, suggesting that there are abundant hydroxyl groups at the CCDs surface. The absorption peak at 2986 cm−1 is assigned to the C–H stretching vibration. The absorption peaks at 1641 cm−1 indicate the presence of carboxyl (CO) functional groups. Moreover, the absorption peak at 1394 cm−1 can be attributable to C–O–C stretching vibration. The absorption peaks at 1077 cm−1 indicate the presence of C-O functional groups. The FTIR reveals that the synthesized CCDs has rich oxygen-containing groups which is shown in Fig. 2(d).

High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscope (HRTEM) analysis was used to predict the surface morphology of the synthesis CCDs. Fig. 3.(a) shows the spherical shape of CCDs with a well-dispersed nature and Fig. 3(b) shows the average size of the CCDs about 3.3 nm and the interplanar spacing is 0.25 nm which is shown in the inside picture of Fig. 3(b).

Iron ions play an important role in biological processes, and an imbalance in their concentration can cause major problems to both environmental and biological systems. Therefore, monitoring the concentration of iron or its presence/absence is crucial. Many techniques are available to detect iron; compared to other techniques, the fluorescence detection (on-off) process is very cost-effective. Fluorescence metal detection or sensing can be a regioselective process (Siahcheshm & Heiden, 2023; Sonaimuthu et al., 2023). The present study investigated the sensing ability of CCDs with various metal ions under the same conditions. The CCDs nanosensor system was tested in the presence of 10 mmol concentrations of freshly prepared metal ion solutions such as Pb2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Cr2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Fe3 +, Fe2+, and Zn2+. These solutions were added to the CCDs solution and kept under UV light at 365 nm. All metal solutions showed a blue colour except for the CCDs mixed with the Fe3+ ion solution because Fe3+ ions quenched the fluorescence of the CCDs. The two primary processes involved in quenching are either the formation of a chelating complex between Fe3+ ions and the surface functional groups of CCDs or the transfer of energy from these surface groups to the d-orbital of the metal atoms. As a result of this complex formation, electrons may transfer from the excited state of CCDs to the d-orbital of Fe3+ ions, leading to a quenching phenomenon For further confirmation, fluorescence spectra were recorded for all metal solutions with a 320 nm excitation wavelength. Surprisingly, the Fe3+ ion emission intensity peak was suppressed compared to other metal ion emission peaks, indicating that Fe3+ ions strongly bind or coordinate with the CCDs' oxygen-containing functional groups such as COOH and OH. Fig. 4(a) shows the fluorescence spectra of all metal solutions, and Fig. 4(b) shows the relationship between F/F0 and the metal ion solution fluorescence intensity. Additionally, different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (0–50 µmol) were prepared to determine the detection limit of Fe3+ ions. Fluorescence spectra were recorded for different concentrations of the Fe3+ ion and CCDs mixture. As the concentration increases, fluorescence intensity decreases due to the quenching or binding nature of CCDs and Fe3+ ions. The quenching nature of CCDs depends on the concentration of the metal ions. Fig. 5(a) shows the fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of Fe3+ ions from 0–50 µmol. The quenching efficiencies of CCDs are explained using a linear relation within a concentration range of 0–50 µmol, following the Stern-Volmer equation, F0/F = 1 + Ksv, where F0 is the fluorescence intensity in the absence of Fe3+ ions, F is the presence of Fe3+ ions, and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant. Fig. 5(b) shows the linearity with correlation coefficients (R2 = 0.9935). The limit of detection is about 4.5 µmol. CCDs can act as fluorescence probe sensors to selectively sense Fe3+ions in an aqueous medium.

The environment and people are severely harmed by methylene blue (MB) dye because it is highly poisonous, non-biodegradable, and carcinogenic. In the textile industry, MB is used as a colouring agent, and unfixing MB solutions are released as toxic pollutants into the environment. The methylene blue degradation/decolourization process is time-effective when using a reduction catalytst. Sodium borohydride is used as a reducing agent in the decolourization process. Fig. 6(a) shows the process without a catalyst. The MB reduction process was monitored using UV–visible spectroscopy. Initially, the MB solution exhibited a broad peak at 665 nm with high-intensity spectra. After 120 min, the intensity peak decreased slightly, indicating a 16 % reduction in methylene blue by NaBH4. The UV–Visible spectra clearly show that NaBH4 alone is not very effective in reduction. When using a catalyst CCDs, the MB absorption intensity peak gradually decreased, and within 22 min, 81 % of methylene blue was effectively reduced (Fig. 6(b)). The UV Vis spectra of the methylene blue reduction process indicate that newly synthesized carbon dots acted as effective catalysts.".

In the study, one-pot and cost-effective microwave methods were adopted to synthesize carbon dots from chayote seed waste. The average size of the particles was about 3.3 nm with a quantum yield of about 9.56 %. CCDs performed as a promising candidate for metal sensing and effective catalysts. CCDs show high selectivity and sensitivity to detect Fe3 + ions in the aqueous medium with a detection limit of about 4.5 µmol. CCDs enhance the rate of the reduction reaction in Methylene Blue dye. 81 % of methylene blue was effectively reduced when using CCDs as a reducing catalyst. The results concluded that biomass-derived carbon dots act as as a promissionf candidate in metal sensor and catalytic applications.

Hema Sindhuja George: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization. Hosimin Selvaraj: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Conceptualization. Andivelu Ilangovan: Writing – review & editing. Bader O Almutairi: resources, Formal analysis, Mohamed Kasim Sheit: Formal analysis, Sathishkumar Kuppusamy: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. Hema Sindhuja George acknowledges the management of Jamal Mohamed College, Tiruchirappalli, Tamilnadu, India. Dr. Hosimin Selvaraj acknowledges Dr. D. S. Kothari postdoctoral fellowship (DSKPDF) and the School of Chemistry, Bharathidasan University, Tiruchirappalli. The authors gratefully acknowledge Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2024R414), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
